chain_name: Noble rot → sugar concentration → sweet wine style

starting_factor.description: Botrytis cinerea infects ripe grapes under alternating morning mist and dry sunny afternoons.

intermediate_steps.description: Botrytis hyphae penetrate grape skin, increasing permeability without destroying the berry.

Under warm, dry afternoon conditions, water evaporates through the perforated skin, shrinking and raisining the grape. This concentrates all dissolved compounds: sugars, acids, glycerol precursors, and flavour compounds.

Botrytis metabolises tartaric acid (via gluconic acid production), partially reducing acidity despite overall concentration. This is a critical nuance: acidity is lower than simple concentration would predict.
High sugar concentration at harvest (potential alcohol 20%+ in Sauternes) combined with retained acidity and botrytis-specific aromatic compounds (honey, apricot, marmalade) defines the style.

final_outcome.description: Intensely sweet wine with high residual sugar, rich glycerol texture, characteristic botrytised aromatics (honey, apricot, marmalade, ginger), and acidity lower than expected from concentration alone.

examples.description: Grapes harvested in multiple tries (tries multiples) at different stages of noble rot development. Resulting wine has ~200 g/L residual sugar, honeyed aromatics, and sufficient acidity for decades of ageing.

Raisined, individually selected botrytised Riesling grapes produce some of the world's most concentrated and long-lived sweet wines. TBA requires exceptional vintages with the right climatic sequence.

related_exam_questions: Explain how noble rot contributes to the style of Sauternes.
What conditions are necessary for noble rot to develop rather than grey rot?
Why do botrytis-affected wines have lower acidity than their sugar concentration would predict?
distinction_note: A Distinction answer specifies the climatic conditions required (alternating mist/sun), the mechanism of concentration (evaporation through perforated skin), the tartaric acid metabolism, and the specific aromatic compounds associated with botrytis — not just 'it concentrates sugars'.
